jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.punchline: His iconic frustrated exclamation
jokes.explanation: Homer's signature catchphrase, perfectly character-integrated and economical. High quotability but not original at this point in the series.

jokes.text: [Gasps] Someone found my keys!
jokes.setup: News interruption creates expectation of important announcement
jokes.punchline: Homer's absurdly personal reaction to generic news intro
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection and character comedy. Homer's self-centered interpretation of news is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: It'll be okay, honey. There's lots more oil where that came from.
jokes.punchline: Homer's tone-deaf reassurance missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Dark environmental humor with Homer's characteristic obliviousness to real issues.

jokes.text: Arr! I'm in a lot of trouble now.
jokes.setup: Reports of drunk captain causing oil spill
jokes.punchline: Captain's casual understatement of massive environmental disaster
jokes.explanation: Sea Captain's characteristic understatement works well but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Hey, I'll give you a hundred bucks to take the blame.
jokes.punchline: Immediately tries to bribe someone else to take blame
jokes.explanation: Quick escalation from admission to attempted bribery shows character's desperation.

jokes.text: As a Golden Globe nominee, I just think it's our duty to make the real globe a little more golden.
jokes.punchline: Contrived pun connecting award show to environmental activism
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay but feels slightly forced. Good satire of celebrity environmental activism.

jokes.text: You can stay right here and give the dog a bath or trim the cat's nails.
jokes.setup: Lisa wants to help clean oil-covered animals
jokes.punchline: Homer suggests cleaning their own neglected pets instead
jokes.explanation: Homer's practical but missing-the-point suggestion is in character and mildly subversive.

jokes.text: You've already used up your birthday and Christmas presents on that peach tree we got you.
jokes.setup: Lisa asking for trip as birthday/Christmas present
jokes.punchline: Reference to previous disappointing gift
jokes.explanation: Mild callback to previous episode establishing Lisa's disappointment with gifts.

jokes.text: [Singsong] La-la, here I am playing on my peach tree, Mom.
jokes.setup: Marge claims Lisa hardly plays with peach tree
jokes.punchline: Lisa's obviously forced demonstration of 'playing' with a tree
jokes.explanation: Lisa's sarcastic compliance shows her intelligence while highlighting the absurd gift.

jokes.text: Cat in the furnace.
jokes.setup: Marge giving detailed pet care instructions
jokes.punchline: Homer's confused misinterpretation combining multiple instructions
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and absurdist logic. Homer's confusion creates dark humor through misunderstanding.

jokes.text: Shall we say... 10:00? All right, just wake me up.
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately delegates responsibility and plans to sleep through it
jokes.explanation: Homer's immediate abdication of parental responsibility is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Dad, do I have to brush my teeth? No, but at least rinse your mouth out with soda.
jokes.punchline: Homer's 'compromise' that's actually worse for dental health
jokes.explanation: Homer's terrible parenting advice creates absurd logic while staying in character.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, but all the animals have already been reserved for celebrities.
jokes.punchline: Environmental cleanup has become celebrity photo opportunity
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of celebrity activism treating disaster cleanup as publicity stunt.

jokes.text: You mean there's nothing left to clean?
jokes.punchline: Lisa's deflation at missing out on environmental work
jokes.explanation: Realistic deflation, shows how celebrity involvement crowds out genuine volunteers

jokes.text: I've got rocks that need washing at home.
jokes.setup: Offered thousands of rocks to wash
jokes.punchline: Marge's practical dismissal comparing to domestic chores
jokes.explanation: Marge's practical response deflates the volunteer coordinator's enthusiasm effectively.

jokes.text: Garbage angels?
jokes.setup: Bart and Homer lying in trash
jokes.punchline: Bart suggests making snow angels in garbage
jokes.explanation: Visual gag combining childhood innocence with environmental destruction. Good contrast.

jokes.text: Do I have to sit up? No. Knock yourself out.
jokes.setup: Bart asking permission to go out
jokes.punchline: Homer's minimal parental engagement
jokes.explanation: Homer's laziness is character-consistent but not particularly original at this point.

jokes.text: Perfectly level flying is the supreme challenge of the scale-model pilot.
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's earnest appreciation of boring precision
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse character moment - nerdy obsession with precision over excitement.

jokes.text: I don't like being outdoors, Smithers. For one thing, there are too many fat children.
jokes.punchline: Casually cruel observation about children's weight
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Burns - casual cruelty delivered with aristocratic disdain. Dark but character-perfect.

jokes.text: It's a secret lab where they take the brains out of zombies and put 'em in the heads of other zombies to create a race of super-zombies.
jokes.punchline: Nelson's elaborate, illogical zombie theory
jokes.explanation: Nelson's over-complicated horror theory shows his bully persona extending to storytelling.

jokes.text: S-U-C-C-E-E-S! That's the way you spell succe-
jokes.punchline: Immediate physical consequence interrupting victory
jokes.explanation: Classic setup-punchline with Bart's premature celebration cut short by consequences.

jokes.text: Are you wearing a grocery bag? I have misplaced my pants.
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual admission of inappropriate attire
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - completely missing the social inappropriateness of his situation.

jokes.text: Yeah, I appreciate the suggestion, lady, but he hates that, and I gotta live with him.
jokes.punchline: Homer prioritizing his own comfort over discipline
jokes.explanation: Homer's selfish parenting logic perfectly captures his character - avoiding conflict over doing right.

jokes.text: You're the man, Homer.
jokes.punchline: Bart's approval of his father's poor parenting
jokes.explanation: Bart's appreciation for being let off the hook shows their dysfunctional but loving relationship.

jokes.text: She makes me taste beer. Come on, boy. Give your old man a little credit.
jokes.setup: Homer asking how Marge usually punishes Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart's sarcastic suggestion of something Homer would enjoy, Homer taking offense
jokes.explanation: Great character interaction - Bart knows his dad's weaknesses, Homer defensive about his drinking.

jokes.text: Lady, I gotta tell ya, I have been grossly misinformed about witches.
jokes.punchline: Discovery that it's actually an elegant burlesque house
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection payoff. Bart's honest reaction to subverted expectations works perfectly.

jokes.text: I prefer not to be called 'man.' My name is Belle, and this is the Maison Derriere. That means the, uh, 'Back House.'
jokes.punchline: Euphemistic French name with crude literal translation
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay establishing the house's nature while maintaining some class through euphemism.

jokes.text: I'll start sorting these bras. [Chuckles]
jokes.punchline: Immediately gravitating toward inappropriate task
jokes.explanation: Age-appropriate humor with Bart's predictable teenage-boy fascination with lingerie.

jokes.text: Ah, the old greet 'n' toss. No problemo.
jokes.setup: Belle explaining door duties: greet visitors and toss troublemakers
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual summarization making it sound routine
jokes.explanation: Bart's casual attitude toward potentially violent bouncer work shows his adaptability.

jokes.text: Does your father know you're working here? It was his idea. In that case, I'll have a whiskey sour.
jokes.setup: Moe's concern about child in burlesque house
jokes.punchline: Immediately accepting it and ordering drink
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe moment - brief moral concern immediately abandoned for personal convenience.

jokes.text: We're having kelp burgers, and we're going to watch a tape of Johnny Arvik. He's the Eskimo comedian.
jokes.setup: Describing evening entertainment at cleanup site
jokes.punchline: Depressingly sparse recreational options
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about the grim reality of volunteer work conditions.

jokes.text: Nudist colonies are everywhere these days. I'd love to go, but I can't get the wrinkles out of my birthday suit.
jokes.setup: Bart doing stand-up routine at burlesque house
jokes.punchline: Pun on 'birthday suit' meaning naked body vs. actual clothing
jokes.explanation: Standard pun-based joke, mildly clever but not exceptional. Fits burlesque house atmosphere.

jokes.text: Adam and Eve must have been the first bookkeepers. They invented the loose-leaf system.
jokes.punchline: Pun on 'loose-leaf' accounting vs. Garden of Eden fig leaves
jokes.explanation: Another standard pun joke. Biblically-themed wordplay fits the adult venue context reasonably well.

jokes.text: If there's a bad bookkeeping joke, I haven't heard it.
jokes.punchline: Self-aware comment on the quality of his own material
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor acknowledging the groan-worthy nature of the preceding jokes. Self-deprecating works.

jokes.text: His dinner is getting all cold and eaten.
jokes.punchline: Inadvertent admission he's eating Bart's dinner
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - expressing concern while simultaneously revealing his selfish behavior. Excellent character comedy.

jokes.text: I have to be up at 6:00 a.m. to swipe Flanders's newspaper.
jokes.setup: Homer saying he needs to get up early
jokes.punchline: Revealing it's for petty theft of neighbor's newspaper
jokes.explanation: Great Homer character moment - treating petty crime as important daily routine. Perfect selfishness.

jokes.text: President Eisenhower celebrates 40th wedding anniversary. Not pictured, Mrs. Eisenhower.
jokes.punchline: Absurd caption noting absence of the person who should be in anniversary photo
jokes.explanation: Absurdist newspaper humor - the obvious absence being noted creates surreal comedy.

jokes.text: I don't care if he was filling in for Mel Zetz.
jokes.punchline: Homer inadvertently referencing the injured emcee he's never heard of
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier setup, but Homer's clueless repetition of the name creates mild humor.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, drugs. You gotta have drugs.
jokes.setup: Belle mentioning drugs as modern parenting challenge
jokes.punchline: Homer's enthusiastic agreement missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Homer completely misunderstanding anti-drug sentiment as pro-drug advice. Perfect character blindness.

jokes.text: This is the National Air and Space Museum, isn't it?
jokes.punchline: Desperate, implausible excuse for being there
jokes.explanation: Skinner's pathetic attempt to maintain dignity while caught in embarrassing situation. Good character moment.

jokes.text: Uh, this isn't gonna be about Jesus, is it?
jokes.punchline: Homer's preemptive attempt to avoid religious discussion
jokes.explanation: Homer's defensive response to Ned shows their established dynamic - anticipating preaching.

jokes.text: All things are about Jesus, Homer, except this.
jokes.punchline: Ned's exception for this particular moral issue
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned response - acknowledging his usual religious focus while making exception for secular moral outrage.

jokes.text: That's true, but I was only in there to get directions on how to get away from there.
jokes.setup: Skinner admitting he was seen there
jokes.punchline: Circular logic excuse that makes no sense
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - asking directions to leave the place you're already in. Perfect Skinner desperation.

jokes.text: If Homer Simpson wants his 10-year-old son working in a burlesque house, then Homer Simpson's 10-year-old son is going to work in a burlesque house!
jokes.punchline: Homer's defiant defense of his terrible parenting decision
jokes.explanation: Homer's stubborn defense of obviously wrong choice shows his pride overriding judgment. Good escalation.

jokes.text: No, I only learned that four days ago.
jokes.setup: Marge asking if he knew it was a burlesque house
jokes.punchline: Homer's honest admission he knew and continued sending Bart
jokes.explanation: Homer's matter-of-fact admission makes his negligence even worse. Perfect character obliviousness.

jokes.text: So, in a way, you really dropped the ball on this one.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining his terrible parenting choice
jokes.punchline: Blaming Marge for his own decision
jokes.explanation: Homer's ability to blame others for his own failures is peak character comedy. Deflection mastery.

jokes.text: I've lived here 52 years. Sixth generation.
jokes.setup: Marge claiming local authority with 37 years, third generation
jokes.punchline: Belle trumping her credentials completely
jokes.explanation: Good escalation comedy with Belle definitively winning the 'who belongs here' argument.

jokes.text: You're about to learn the two most dangerous words in the English language are 'Marge Simpson.'
jokes.punchline: Building up Marge as a formidable opponent despite her mild demeanor
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Marge as unlikely antagonist. Builds dramatic tension while maintaining character.

jokes.text: But this time it's not about that giant inflatable Dos Equis bottle.
jokes.punchline: Reference to previous moral crusade
jokes.explanation: Establishes Marge's history of moral crusades. Mild callback humor building character consistency.

jokes.text: What's wrong with this house? Is it the plumbin'? No. Well, then quit bad-mouthing the house!
jokes.punchline: Citizens focusing on literal house problems, not moral issues
jokes.explanation: Good misunderstanding comedy with townspeople's literal interpretation of Marge's complaint.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm afraid this problem goes far beyond Eugene and Rusty.
jokes.punchline: Marge revealing she has a comprehensive list of customers
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Marge's thorough investigation. Sets up the revelation sequence.

jokes.text: Julius! Clancy! Skinner! Seymour! Mother. Patty? Cletus!
jokes.setup: Marge reveals photos of burlesque house patrons
jokes.punchline: Rapid-fire reveals of Springfield authority figures and unexpected customers
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating reveal sequence exposing hypocrisy across Springfield's leadership

jokes.text: My parents insisted I give it a try, sir.
jokes.setup: Burns discovering Smithers at burlesque house
jokes.punchline: Smithers' pathetic excuse blaming his parents
jokes.explanation: Perfect Smithers - trying to maintain dignity while caught in embarrassing heterosexual situation.

jokes.text: Are they talkin' about the bordello? No, the burlesque house. So just keep your mouth shut.
jokes.punchline: Cletus confusing establishments, Brandine's damage control
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Cletus's confusion and their family dynamics.

jokes.text: No, there's no justice like angry-mob justice.
jokes.setup: Mob forming to tear down house
jokes.punchline: Moe's enthusiastic endorsement of vigilante violence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe - embracing violent mob mentality with characteristic dark enthusiasm.

jokes.text: Don't you call me that. I'm an entrepreneuse.
jokes.punchline: Correction to fancier French term for same thing
jokes.explanation: Belle maintaining her class and dignity through linguistic sophistication. Character consistent.

jokes.text: We could tear it down, but we'd be tearing down a part of ourselves.
jokes.punchline: Unexpectedly profound and persuasive argument from Homer
jokes.explanation: Rare moment of Homer wisdom setting up the musical number. Good character subversion.

jokes.text: Well, I'm convinced. The house stays.
jokes.punchline: Homer's simple conclusion after complex performance
jokes.explanation: Homer's matter-of-fact conclusion after elaborate musical sequence. Good economy and character consistency.

jokes.text: It was a very convincing song, Marge. Mm-hmm, there were kicks and everything.
jokes.setup: Marge questioning how song changed their minds
jokes.punchline: Citizens citing superficial entertainment value over moral arguments
jokes.explanation: Good commentary on how easily swayed people are by entertainment over logic. Observational humor.

jokes.text: Thanks a lot, Marge. That was our only burlesque house.
jokes.punchline: Barney's complaint about loss of entertainment venue
jokes.explanation: Barney's priorities showing through his complaint. Character-consistent focus on lost entertainment.

jokes.text: What did you name him? Chip.
jokes.setup: Joke about Twiggy and Woody having baby
jokes.punchline: Wood-themed pun name
jokes.explanation: Standard pun joke in closing performance. Functional but predictable wood-themed wordplay.
